concepts.title: Sign of quadratic expression
concepts.conceptBody:
Sign of Quadratic Expression

Let $f(x)=a{{x}^{2}}+bx+c$ or $y=a{{x}^{2}}+bx+c$

Where a , b , c ∈ R and a ≠ 0, for some values of x , f ( x ) may be positive, negative or zero. This gives the following cases :

(i) a > 0 and D < 0, so $f(x)>0$ for all $x\in R$ i.e. , $f(x)$ is positive for all real values of x .

(ii) a < 0 and D < 0, so $f(x)<0$ for all x ∈ R i.e. , f ( x ) is negative for all real values of x .

(iii) a > 0 and D = 0, so $f(x)\ge 0$ for all x ∈ R i.e. , f ( x ) is positive for all real values of x except at vertex, where $f(x)=0$.

(iv) a < 0 and D = 0, so $f(x)\le 0$ for all x ∈ R i.e. f ( x ) is negative for all real values of x except at vertex, where $f(x)=0$.

(v) a > 0 and D > 0, let $f(x)=0$ have two real roots α and β $(\alpha <\beta )$, then $f(x)>0$ for all $x\in (-\infty ,\,\alpha )\cup (\beta ,\,\infty )$ and $f(x)<0$ for all $x\in (\alpha ,\,\beta )$.

(vi) $a<0$ and $D>0$, let $f(x)=0$ have two real roots α and β $(\alpha <\beta )$. Then $f(x)<0$ for all $x\in (-\infty ,\,\alpha )\cup (\beta ,\,\infty )$ and $f(x)>0$ for all $x\in (\alpha ,\,\beta )$

concepts.title: Quadratic always positive or always negative
concepts.conceptBody:
Graph of a Quadratic Expression

We have $y=a{{x}^{2}}+bx+c=f(x)$

$y=a\left[ {{\left( x+\frac{b}{2a} \right)}^{2}}-\frac{D}{4{{a}^{2}}} \right]$⇒ $y+\frac{D}{4a}=a{{\left( x+\frac{b}{2a} \right)}^{2}}$

Now, let $y+\frac{D}{4a}=Y$ and $X=x+\frac{b}{2a}$

$Y=a.{{X}^{2}}$ ⇒ ${{X}^{2}}=\frac{1}{a}Y$

(i) The graph of the curve $y=f(x)$ is parabolic.

(ii) The axis of parabola is $X=0$ or $x+\frac{b}{2a}=0$

i.e. (parallel to y- axis).

(iii) (a) If a > 0, then the parabola opens upward.

(b) If a < 0, then the parabola opens downward.

 

(iv) Intersection with axis

(a) Intersection with x- axis : For x axis, $y=0$

⇒ $a{{x}^{2}}+bx+c=0$ ⇒ $x=\frac{-b\pm \sqrt{D}}{2a}$

For D > 0, parabola cuts x- axis in two real and distinct points i.e. $x=\frac{-b\pm \sqrt{D}}{2a}$.

For D =0,parabola touches x- axis in one point, $x=-b/2a$.

 

For D < 0, parabola does not cut x- axis ( i.e. imaginary value of x ).

 

(b) Intersection with axis y -axis : For y axis $x=0$, $y=c$.

concepts.title: Descrete's Rule of Signs
concepts.conceptBody:
Descrete's Rule of Sign

The maximum number of positive real roots of a polynomial equation $f(x)=0$ is the number of changes of sign from positive to negative and negative to positive in f ( x ).

The maximum number of negative real roots of a polynomial equation $f(x)=0$ is the number of changes of sign from positive to negative and negative to positive in $f(-x)$.

concepts.title: Solving inequality based on coefficients
concepts.conceptBody:
Algebraic Inequalities

If P ( x ) and Q ( x ) are polynomial in x , then the inequation

$\frac{P(x)}{Q(x)}>0,\,\frac{P(x)}{Q(x)}<0,\,\frac{P(x)}{Q(x)}\ge 0$ and $\frac{P(x)}{Q(x)}\le 0$

are known as rational algebraic inequations.

To solve these inequations we use the sign method as explained in the following algorithm.

Algorithm 

Step I: Obtain P ( x ) and Q ( x ).

Step II: Factorize P ( x ) and Q ( x ) into linear factors.

Step III: Make the coefficient of x positive in all factors.

Step IV: Obtain critical points by equating all factors to zero.

Step V: Plot the critical points on the number line. If there are n critical points, they divide the number line into ( n + 1) regions.

Step VI: In the right most region the expression $\frac{P(x)}{Q(x)}$ bears positive sign and in other regions the expression bears positive and negative signs depending on the exponents of the factors.

examples.title: The adjoining figure shows the graph of $y=a x^2+b x+c$. Then
If the graph of the function $y=(a-b)^2 x^2+2(a+b-2 c) x+1(\forall a \neq b)$ is strictly above the $x$-axis, then
The least integral value of a for which the graphs $y=2 a x+1$ and $y=(a-6) x^2-2$ do not intersect is
Which of the following is a possible graph for the quadratic equation $y=x^2-$ $4 x+4 ?$
Consider the quadratic equation $2 x^2-5 x+2=0$. What can you conclude about the graph of this equation?
For what values of $k$ does the quadratic equation $x^2-2 k x+k+3=0$ have both roots with the same sign?
What is the sign of the quadratic equation $3 x^2-7 x+2$ for $x\gt1$ ?
